- **Boswell et al. (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon-hangover effect"**: job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that typically fades within a year, returning toward baseline. Worth knowing when evaluating how a new job *feels* in your imagination.

The Meta offer isn't just money-vs-interest; it's interest-vs-growth, where the research is much less one-sided.
2. **Management itself is mixed.** Studies show first-line managers report more stress and time pressure, but also often more meaning and autonomy. Satisfaction depends heavily on whether the person genuinely wanted the role versus drifted into it — you apparently genuinely want it.

## Honest synthesis

The literature would predict: the salary difference will affect your happiness very little within a year. The interestingness of the work will keep mattering indefinitely. The open question — which research can't settle — is whether "leading a team" is a deep, self-concordant goal for you (in which case forgoing it is the kind of inaction people regret) or an attractive-sounding status marker (in which case it follows the same adaptation curve as salary). - I'm recalling citations from memory — the broad findings are solid, but verify specific numbers before relying on them.

answer:
Great question, and there's actually a decent body of research touching on this. Here's what I can recall, organized by theme:

## Intrinsic interest vs. pay and satisfaction

- **Judge et al. (2010)** did a meta-analysis on the relationship between pay and job satisfaction and found a surprisingly weak correlation (~0.15). Pay level explains very little variance in how satisfied people are with their jobs once you're above a comfortable threshold.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found that emotional well-being plateaus around a certain income level (~$75k at the time, higher adjusted today). At an OpenAI Research Engineer salary, you're far past the point where marginal income reliably buys day-to-day happiness. (Note: Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up complicated this — well-being may keep rising with log income — but the effect size per dollar at high incomes is small.)
- The **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)** is one of the most replicated frameworks in organizational psychology: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict intrinsic motivation and satisfaction much more strongly than compensation does.
- **Nye et al. (2012)** meta-analysis on vocational interests found that interest-job fit predicts not just satisfaction but also performance and persistence — people in work that interests them do better and stay longer.

## Hedonic adaptation

- A salary bump is one of the things people adapt to fastest. **Clark et al.** and the broader "hedonic treadmill" literature (going back to Brickman's lottery winner studies, 1978) show that the satisfaction boost from a raise largely fades within roughly a year. Daily experience of work — including whether it's engaging — is much more resistant to adaptation, because you re-experience it every day.
- **Boyce et al. (2010)** found that *relative* income (rank among peers) matters more than absolute income, which cuts both ways for your situation.

## Regret research — the important nuance for you

This is where it gets interesting, because the literature doesn't unambiguously say "stay with the interesting job":

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** found that in the long run, people regret *inactions* (things they didn't try) more than actions. Short-term, we regret mistakes we made; long-term, we regret roads not taken.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)** found people's most enduring regrets concern failures to live up to their *ideal self* (aspirations, who they wanted to become) rather than their *ought self* (duties, obligations).

You said leading a team is "something I always wanted." That's exactly the kind of thing the regret literature flags — unfulfilled aspirations tend to nag for decades, whereas trying it and discovering you don't like it tends to be a regret that fades.

## Meaning and "calling" orientation

- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** showed that people who see their work as a "calling" (vs. a job or career) report higher life satisfaction — and this orientation is partly about the work itself, partly about how you frame it.
- The **Self-Determination Theory** literature (Deci & Ryan) consistently finds that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive sustained motivation. Notably, *leading a team* can feed those needs too (competence in a new domain, relatedness) — "interesting" isn't the only path to intrinsic motivation.

## One caution about your own forecasting

**Schkade & Kahneman's "focusing illusion"** (2003): whatever factor you're currently focused on (interestingness, salary, the title) will feel more important than it actually will be in daily life. When evaluating, try to predict your typical *Tuesday afternoon* in each job, not the abstract comparison.

## Honest summary

The research tilts toward interesting work mattering more than the salary differential — pay adapts, daily engagement doesn't. But the regret literature complicates it: if leading a team is a genuine long-held aspiration (not just a status marker), never trying it is the classic profile of long-term regret. The empirically least-regrettable framing might be: is this *the right opportunity* to try leadership, or could you get that at OpenAI or elsewhere later, with work you also find interesting?

One caveat: I'm recalling these citations from memory, so verify the specifics before relying on them — but the broad findings (weak pay-satisfaction link, hedonic adaptation to income, inaction regret) are well-established and have held up across replications.

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** is the big framework here: well-being at work is driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Intrinsically motivating work strongly predicts sustained engagement and well-being; extrinsic rewards predict it weakly and can even undermine intrinsic motivation (the "overjustification effect," though its size is debated).
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)** meta-analysis: intrinsic motivation is a solid predictor of performance and persistence, especially for quality of work (as opposed to quantity), and incentives don't substitute for it.
- **Wrzesniewski et al.** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction and lower absenteeism than those oriented toward pay or advancement.
- **Interest congruence** research (Holland's framework; meta-analyses by **Nye et al., 2012, 2017**): fit between personal interests and job content correlates with satisfaction modestly (~.10–.20) but more strongly with performance and persistence in the field. Worth noting: the satisfaction effect is *smaller* than folk wisdom suggests.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions — "the road not taken" looms larger over time. This cuts both ways for you: not taking the Meta offer (a leadership opportunity you've always wanted) and leaving interesting work could each become an "inaction regret" depending on framing.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career and education are among the top life-regret domains, and regrets cluster where people perceived *opportunity* — i.e., people most regret not pursuing paths they felt were genuinely available.

## The factor research can't settle for you

One complication: you're not just trading interest vs. money. The leadership role touches on **self-concordant goals** (**Sheldon & Elliot, 1999**) — pursuing goals aligned with long-held personal values produces lasting well-being gains, unlike goals adopted for external reasons. If leading a team is a genuinely self-concordant goal for you, that's the one factor in the Meta offer the research suggests *could* rival intrinsic interest in the work itself.

## Summary of what the literature implies

- The salary bump will likely contribute almost nothing to your happiness after ~6–12 months.
- Intrinsic interest in the work is one of the more durable predictors of sustained satisfaction and performance.
- The genuinely open question is the leadership goal: if it's a deep, long-standing aspiration, regret research suggests not trying it could sting more over time than the work being less interesting — but only if the desire is authentically yours rather than a status marker.

If you want to dig in, Judge et al. (2010), Cerasoli et al. (2014), and the Killingsworth/Kahneman adversarial collaboration (2023) are good starting points.

answer:
Great question — there's actually a fair amount of relevant research. Here's what I can recall, organized by topic. (Caveat: I'm citing from memory, so verify exact numbers before relying on them.)

## Pay vs. satisfaction

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak — around **r = 0.15**. People at high pay levels were barely more satisfied with their jobs than people earning much less.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around $75k household income. **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged this with experience-sampling data showing continued gains with log(income), and their **2023 adversarial collaboration** (Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers) reconciled it: income keeps helping for most people, but with strongly diminishing returns. At an OpenAI Research Engineer salary, you're deep in the flat part of the curve — a "bit higher" salary will likely be nearly undetectable in your well-being.
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (e.g., Clark et al. on income changes) shows satisfaction boosts from raises largely fade within roughly a year. Relative income and comparisons matter more than absolute level (Boyce et al., 2010).

## Interesting/meaningful work vs. satisfaction

- The **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976) — one of the most replicated frameworks in I/O psychology — finds that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict intrinsic motivation and satisfaction substantially more strongly than compensation.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan): intrinsic motivation (autonomy, competence, relatedness) predicts sustained engagement and well-being; extrinsic rewards don't, and can even crowd out intrinsic motivation.
- **Hu & Hirsh (2017)** found people were willing to accept substantially lower salaries (in their studies, roughly 30%+ lower) for work they considered meaningful, and that meaningfulness predicted satisfaction better than income.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on job/career/calling orientations: people who experience their work as a "calling" report higher job and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement — even within the same occupation.
- However: **interest-congruence meta-analyses** (Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, 2017) found interest fit predicts performance and persistence well, but its correlation with *satisfaction* is more modest than people assume. Interest matters, but it's not everything.

## Regret research — relevant to your "would I regret it" framing

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* (chances not taken) far more than actions. Short-term, action regrets sting more; long-term, "what if I had tried" dominates.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career is consistently the **#1 regret domain** in American adults' lives — and the dominant career regrets involve not pursuing opportunities and unfulfilled potential, not failed attempts.

## A few complications worth noting

1. **Your situation cuts both ways on the regret literature.** "Leading a team is something I always wanted" is exactly the kind of unacted-upon desire that produces long-term regret. The research on interesting work doesn't obviously dominate here — leadership, if it's a genuine aspiration, feeds the same intrinsic-motivation needs (competence, growth, impact on people).
2. **Interest is partly constructed, not just discovered.** O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) found that a "growth mindset of interest" predicts developing engagement in initially less-interesting domains. **Job crafting** research (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001) shows people actively reshape roles to make them more engaging — and a team lead arguably has *more* latitude to do that.
3. **Management transitions are a mixed bag empirically** — first-time managers often report a temporary well-being dip, and satisfaction depends heavily on whether the leadership motivation is genuine ("motivation to lead," Chan & Drasgow, 2001) versus status-driven.

## Bottom line from the literature

The research stacks up roughly like this: the salary delta will almost certainly not matter for your happiness; interesting work reliably does matter; but unfulfilled aspirations are the single biggest source of long-term regret. So the real empirical question isn't "interesting work vs. money" — it's "interesting work vs. a genuinely held leadership aspiration," and the literature doesn't give a clean winner there. It depends on whether "leading a team" is a deep motivation or an inherited status goal — which the research can't answer for you.

## On regret

## Pay and job satisfaction: weaker link than people expect

## Intrinsic interest in the work: stronger and more durable link

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of studies show that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically motivated work predict sustained engagement and well-being better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: task significance, skill variety, and task identity (roughly, "is the work interesting and meaningful") are robust predictors of internal motivation and satisfaction.

This is maybe the most relevant cluster for your situation:

- **Hsee and colleagues ("lay rationalism")**: when *choosing*, people systematically overweight quantifiable, justifiable attributes (salary, title) and underweight experiential ones (how engaging the daily work is) — even though the experiential attributes better predict how they'll actually feel afterward.
- **Kahneman's focusing illusion**: "Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it." A salary difference is highly salient during the decision but barely registers in daily experience; the texture of your daily work registers constantly.

## Regret research

## Interest and work characteristics

- The **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976) is the classic framework: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict intrinsic motivation and satisfaction robustly across decades of replications. "How interesting is the work" loads heavily on these.

## Honest summary of what the literature implies

The salary difference should get near-zero weight. The real question the research can't answer for you is whether "leading a team" is a genuine intrinsic aspiration or a status/extrinsic one — the literature predicts very different outcomes for those two cases. ## Intrinsic motivation and meaningful work

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically interesting work predict sustained well-being and engagement far better than extrinsic rewards. There's also evidence that extrinsic incentives can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation.

- **Allan et al. (2019), meta-analysis** on meaningful work: strong associations with engagement, commitment, and life satisfaction; meaningfulness was one of the strongest predictors of overall job attitudes.
- **Cassar & Meier (2018)** in *Journal of Economic Perspectives* reviewed economic evidence that people demonstrably sacrifice significant pay for meaning, and treat meaning as a stable source of utility rather than something they adapt to.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* far more than actions. This cuts both ways for you — it argues against turning down something just from inertia, but it applies to *whichever* path represents the thing you'd otherwise never try.

1. Is leading a team a genuinely self-concordant goal for you, or an introjected "should"?

**Summary of what the literature predicts**: the salary difference will have near-zero lasting effect; the interestingness difference will have a large, durable effect; the leadership opportunity could matter a lot, but only if it's a genuine intrinsic goal rather than a status marker — and a leadership path may also open at your current employer, which would dominate both options.

## One more relevant bias

- **Schkade & Kahneman (1998)** on the **focusing illusion**: "Nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you are thinking about it." The literature points fairly consistently in one direction: marginal salary at high income levels has small, fast-adapting effects, while work interest and intrinsic engagement have larger, durable effects. ## The honest synthesis

The research pattern is fairly consistent: if this were *interesting work vs. salary*, the literature would clearly favor staying. The salary delta will hedonically adapt away; interesting work doesn't.

answer:
There's actually a decent body of research relevant to your decision. Here's what I can recall, organized by question:

## Does pay buy job satisfaction / happiness?

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of 86 studies, found the correlation between salary and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). People earning more are barely more satisfied with their jobs than people earning less, and pay *level* matters less than perceived pay *fairness*.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k; **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged this, showing continued (log-linear) gains. Their **adversarial collaboration (Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023)** reconciled it: more money keeps helping for most people, but the effect flattens for an unhappy minority — and the marginal effect at high incomes is small. At OpenAI/Meta compensation levels, a modest salary bump is unlikely to move your well-being much.
- **Hedonic adaptation** is well documented for pay raises specifically: the satisfaction boost largely fades within about a year.

## Does intrinsic interest matter?

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** has decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation — interest, autonomy, sense of mastery — predicts sustained well-being, engagement, and persistence far better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence extrinsic rewards can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (2014)**, studying ~11,000 West Point cadets, found those with internal motives had better long-term outcomes, and holding instrumental motives *alongside* internal ones actually predicted worse outcomes — mixed motives didn't add up.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis: meaningful work correlates strongly with life satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout — stronger relationships than pay shows.
- Caveat for balance: **interest congruence** research (e.g., Nye et al., 2012; Tsabari et al., 2005) finds the link between vocational interest fit and satisfaction is real but more modest than people assume (often r ≈ .10–.20). Interest matters, but it's not everything.

## What about job changes and regret?

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the "honeymoon–hangover effect": job satisfaction spikes after a voluntary job change, then declines back toward baseline within a year or two. Worth knowing whichever way you decide.
- **Boyce & Oswald (2012)** found that being promoted to a higher role brought *no* improvement in health and an *increase* in mental strain — relevant to the management piece.
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* (chances not taken) more than actions. **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career is among the top life regret domains, particularly where people felt they had unexploited opportunity.

## One complication specific to your case

The regret and motivation literature cuts both ways here, because your situation isn't cleanly "interesting work vs. money." You said leading a team is *something you always wanted* — that's not an extrinsic motive. **Sheldon & Elliot's (1999) self-concordance model** shows that progress toward goals aligned with your authentic values does boost well-being, while progress toward goals you pursue for external reasons doesn't. So the research-grounded question for you is arguably: is "leading a team" a self-concordant goal of yours, or a status goal you've absorbed? And note the Gilovich inaction-regret finding applies to *both* options — never trying management, or leaving uniquely interesting work.

The broad pattern, if I had to compress it: interesting/meaningful work and autonomy are more reliable predictors of sustained satisfaction than incremental pay at your income level; management promotions per se don't reliably improve well-being; but attaining a genuinely self-concordant long-held goal does.

Happy to dig into any of these studies in more detail if one seems especially relevant.

## Pay vs. intrinsic interest

**Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak — around r = .15. People earning very different salaries report similar job satisfaction once basic needs are met. A salary bump that's "a bit higher" is unlikely to move your happiness much.

**Hedonic adaptation** research (Frederick & Loewenstein, and Lucas's longitudinal work) consistently shows people adapt to income increases within months to a couple of years. The salary delta stops registering; the daily texture of the work doesn't.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found emotional well-being plateaus around a certain income level (famously ~$75K then; Killingsworth's 2021 follow-up complicated this, but the effect of marginal income at high salaries is small either way). At OpenAI/Meta compensation levels, you're far past the steep part of the curve.

## Does interesting work actually predict happiness?

**Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** has decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation — autonomy, competence, and interest in the work itself — predicts well-being, engagement, and persistence far better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence that extrinsic rewards can *crowd out* intrinsic motivation (the overjustification effect), though that's debated for high-skill work.

**Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on vocational interest fit found that interest–job fit predicts both performance and persistence, with effects stronger than many "classic" predictors.

**Amabile & Kramer's "progress principle"** (diary studies of knowledge workers) found that the single strongest predictor of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work — not recognition or incentives.

**Wrzesniewski's work on "calling" orientation** shows people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or even "career" (advancement) orientations.

## What about regret?

**Roese & Summerville (2005)** meta-analysis: career is the #1 regret domain in life (ahead of romance, education, parenting).

**Gilovich & Medvec (1994)**: in the short term people regret actions; in the long term they overwhelmingly regret *inactions* and forgone opportunities — "the path not taken." This cuts both ways for you, though: the management opportunity is also a path.

## The complication that's relevant to your case

Your situation isn't purely "interesting work vs. money" — it's interesting work vs. **a leadership role you've always wanted**. That matters because:

- SDT's "competence" and "growth" needs can be fulfilled by mastering management, not just research.
- **Herzberg's two-factor theory**: salary is a "hygiene factor" (its absence demotivates, its presence doesn't motivate), but *responsibility and advancement* are genuine motivators — same category as interesting work.
- Research on managerial transitions (e.g., work summarized by Boyatzis, and Gallup's engagement data) shows outcomes are bimodal: people who genuinely want to manage often thrive; people who drift into it for status/pay often regret it.

## Honest summary

The literature is fairly one-sided that **salary alone is a poor reason to switch** at your income level. The evidence on intrinsic interest is strong: it predicts sustained satisfaction and is resistant to hedonic adaptation in a way money isn't. ## What does predict satisfaction: the nature of the work itself

- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as a "calling" report significantly higher life satisfaction and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement — *even within the same occupation*.

## Honest synthesis of what the literature implies

This is directly relevant to your situation: the novelty and the raise wear off; the day-to-day content of the work doesn't.

## Intrinsic interest predicts satisfaction, persistence, and performance

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**, a meta-analysis of 40 years of research, found intrinsic motivation is a moderate-to-strong predictor of performance, and it predicts *quality* of work better than incentives do (incentives predict quantity better).
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)** found that interest–job fit predicts both performance and persistence/tenure — people in work that matches their interests stay longer and do better.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; meta-analyzed by Van den Broeck et al., 2016) consistently shows that satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs predicts well-being and engagement far better than compensation does.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)**: people who see their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher job *and life* satisfaction than those who see it as a career (advancement) or job (paycheck) — even within the same occupation.

## What people regret

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. Short-term, regrettable actions sting more; over years, "the road not taken" dominates.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career is one of the top life regret domains.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**: people's most enduring regrets concern failures to live up to their *ideal* self (dreams, aspirations) rather than their *ought* self (duties, sensible obligations).

## The important caveat for your case

The regret and self-concordance research cuts both ways for you. **Sheldon & Elliot (1999)** showed that pursuing goals aligned with your authentic values ("self-concordant goals") is what drives well-being gains. You said leading a team is *something you've always wanted* — that's not an extrinsic factor like salary; that's an ideal-self aspiration. If you decline and never get to lead, the inaction-regret literature applies to *that* choice too.

So the honest research-based summary:

1. The **salary difference will likely have near-zero long-term effect** on your happiness — that's one of the most robust findings here.
2. **Interesting work is a strong, durable predictor** of satisfaction and persistence — don't discount it.

- **Wrzesniewski et al.** found people who view work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* far more than actions — and **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career is one of the top two life regret domains.

## The complication in your case

The regret literature actually cuts both ways for you, and it's worth being honest about that. You said leading a team is *"something I always wanted"* — that's an ideal-self aspiration too. The research doesn't cleanly say "stay with interesting work"; it says **avoid the choice you'd frame as the inaction/unfulfilled-dream later**. Two more relevant findings:

## Bottom line from the literature

If you compress it: pay differences at your level predict almost nothing about future happiness; intrinsic interest predicts a lot; and the deepest regrets come from abandoned aspirations — which means the real question isn't "interesting vs. money," it's "which of these is closer to your genuine aspiration: this research, or leading people?" answer:

## Pay matters less than people expect

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 samples, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is only about **r = .15**. Even the correlation between pay and *satisfaction with pay itself* was weak (~.23). People at very different salary levels report surprisingly similar satisfaction.
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around $75k. **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged this, finding a log-linear relationship with no plateau, and their adversarial collaboration (**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**) landed in between — but the key point for you: the effect is *logarithmic*. Going from one high tech salary to a slightly higher one buys very little well-being.
- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon-hangover effect"**: job satisfaction spikes after a voluntary job change, then declines back toward baseline within about a year. Hedonic adaptation to raises is fast; adaptation to the *content* of daily work is much slower.

## Interest and intrinsic motivation predict satisfaction and performance

- **Herzberg's two-factor theory** (old but repeatedly supported in spirit): pay is a "hygiene factor" — its absence causes dissatisfaction, but it doesn't generate lasting satisfaction. Interesting work, achievement, and responsibility are the actual "motivators."
- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: well-being at work depends on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)** meta-analysis: intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially *quality* of work (extrinsic incentives predict quantity better).
- **Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses: fit between vocational interests and the job predicts performance and persistence better than previously believed.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)**: people who see their work as a "calling" report substantially higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations.

## Regret research cuts both ways for you

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: long-term regrets are overwhelmingly about *inaction* — things not tried — rather than actions taken.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**: the most persistent regrets concern failures to live up to one's *ideal self* (aspirations, dreams) rather than one's "ought self" (duties). This is important for you because **leading a team is something you "always wanted"** — that's an ideal-self aspiration too, not just a salary bump. Forgoing it could be exactly the kind of thing this literature says people regret.

## One caution about the management piece

- **Boyce & Oswald (2012)** and **Johnston & Lee (2013)** found that promotions to supervisory roles produce a short-term satisfaction boost but *worse mental health* and declining satisfaction afterward — increased strain without lasting well-being gains. Wanting to lead and enjoying leading are not always the same thing; the research suggests testing this assumption matters.

## How the literature maps to your decision

The research is fairly one-sided that **the salary difference will likely be hedonically irrelevant within a year**, while **interest in the daily work is one of the strongest durable predictors of satisfaction**. The genuinely open question is the leadership dimension: if leading a team is a real ideal-self aspiration, the regret literature suggests not trying it carries its own long-term cost — but the promotion literature warns that the anticipated satisfaction from management roles is often overestimated.

One practical implication: the cleanest research-backed move might be to seek the leadership experience *without* giving up interesting work — e.g., exploring whether a path to leading a small team exists where you are now.

answer:

## Pay and satisfaction

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak — around r = .15. Pay satisfaction correlated with actual pay at only ~.23. People at very different income levels within similar jobs report similar satisfaction.

- Research on **hedonic adaptation** (Frederick & Loewenstein and others) consistently shows people adapt to pay raises within months; the satisfaction bump from a raise largely evaporates within about a year.

## Intrinsic interest and work characteristics

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of research showing autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive sustained motivation and well-being far more than extrinsic rewards. Intrinsic motivation also predicts persistence and creative performance.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)** meta-analysis: interest–job fit predicts performance and persistence, with meaningful effect sizes — fit matters not just for feeling good but for doing well.
- **Wrzesniewski et al.** on work orientations: people who view their work as a "calling" rather than a "job" or "career" report higher life and work satisfaction, fewer sick days.
- **Amabile & Kramer's "progress principle"**: the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work — not compensation or recognition.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. Career-related regrets are heavily weighted toward chances not taken.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career and education are the top two life domains for regret, and regret concentrates where people perceive foregone opportunity.

## The honest complication for your case

The literature would push toward "interesting work wins" — *except* your Meta offer isn't purely extrinsic. Leading a team is something you say you've always wanted, which makes it a competence/growth opportunity in SDT terms, not just money. There's research (e.g., on unfulfilled managerial aspirations) suggesting that chronically unmet goals also corrode satisfaction. So the regret-of-inaction finding cuts both ways here: you could regret leaving interesting work, or regret never trying leadership.

One more relevant finding: Kahneman's **focusing illusion** — "nothing in life is as important as you think it is while you're thinking about it." Whichever factor you're weighing right now (salary, title, team) will likely matter less day-to-day than you currently expect; the texture of the daily work tends to dominate lived experience.

If I had to summarize the literature in one line: **interestingness of work and intrinsic fit predict long-run satisfaction much better than moderate pay differences do, but unpursued aspirations are a major source of regret** — so the real question is whether team leadership is a genuine aspiration or a status marker, and whether you could get it at your current employer eventually.

A "bit higher" salary at that range is unlikely to register in well-being data.
- **Hedonic adaptation**: raises produce a happiness bump that fades within about a year (Clark et al., 2008; Diener et al., 2006).

## Interest and intrinsic motivation

- **Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on *interest congruence* (fit between your interests and your work) found it predicts both performance and persistence, with meaningful effect sizes — interest fit is one of the better predictors of long-term success and tenure in a role.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan): decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically motivated work predict sustained well-being, while extrinsic rewards (pay, status) show weaker and less durable effects. - **Boswell et al. (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon–hangover effect"**: job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that typically declines back toward baseline within a year. If the new job's underlying content is less satisfying, the hangover can land below where you started.

- **Schkade & Kahneman (1998)** on the **focusing illusion**: whatever factor you're currently focused on (interestingness, leadership) gets overweighted in predictions of future happiness relative to its actual impact.

## Rough synthesis of the literature

The evidence pattern is fairly consistent: **a small salary difference at high income levels predicts almost nothing; interesting/meaningful work is one of the strongest and most durable predictors of satisfaction.** The genuinely uncertain variable in your case is the leadership opportunity — if it's an authentic, intrinsically motivated goal, the research suggests it matters in the same way "interesting work" does. answer:
There's actually a fair amount of relevant research here. Let me summarize the main findings, with caveats where the evidence is mixed.

## Pay and job satisfaction: weaker link than people expect

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of 92 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is only about **r = .15**. Pay differences explain a surprisingly small fraction of how satisfied people are at work.
- **Herzberg's two-factor theory** (older but influential): pay acts as a "hygiene factor" — low pay creates dissatisfaction, but high pay doesn't generate lasting satisfaction. Interesting work, achievement, and growth are "motivators" that do.
- **Hedonic adaptation**: people adapt to salary increases fairly quickly (Boyce, Wood et al.; the broader adaptation literature). The bump in happiness from a raise tends to fade within a year or two, whereas the day-to-day experience of your work doesn't adapt away in the same fashion.
- **Kahneman et al. (2006)** on the "focusing illusion": people systematically overestimate how much income affects happiness, partly because when comparing options, salary is salient and quantifiable while daily experience is not. This is directly relevant to comparing job offers — the comparison process itself biases you toward the measurable factor.

## Intrinsic interest and meaningful work: strong link

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of research showing that autonomy, competence, and intrinsic motivation predict well-being and sustained engagement, while extrinsically motivated work predicts burnout and disengagement.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (2014)**, a longitudinal study of ~11,000 West Point cadets: those with primarily intrinsic motives had better long-term outcomes; notably, holding *both* strong intrinsic and instrumental (e.g., money/status) motives predicted *worse* outcomes than intrinsic motives alone.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis: perceived meaningfulness of work correlates very strongly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .60–.70 range) — one of the largest effects in the organizational literature.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: skill variety, task significance, and autonomy are robust predictors of satisfaction and motivation.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* (chances not taken) more than actions. This cuts in an interesting direction for you — but the "action not taken" could be either offer, including the leadership opportunity.
- Career-specific regret surveys consistently show that "took a job mainly for the money" is among the most commonly reported career regrets, while "chose meaningful work over higher pay" rarely is.

## The complication in your case: the management role

This is where it's not clean-cut for you:

- **Sheldon & Elliot's self-concordance research**: pursuing goals that align with your authentic values (you say you've *always wanted* to lead a team) predicts well-being gains. If leadership is genuinely intrinsic for you, that's not just an "extrinsic" factor.
- However, **Johnston & Lee (2013)** found that promotions into managerial roles improve job satisfaction briefly but increase strain and worsen mental health within a few years. And the transition from individual contributor to manager often reduces exactly the things SDT says matter (time on craft, sense of competence) — at least initially.

## Honest caveats

- Most of this is correlational; people who do interesting work differ in many ways from those who don't.
- Effect sizes are population averages. The pay–satisfaction correlation being weak doesn't mean it's weak *for you*, especially if the money unlocks something concrete (housing, family plans).
- "Interesting" isn't stable — work that's fascinating today can become routine, and a "boring" domain with great colleagues and autonomy can become engaging. The research suggests autonomy, growth, and people may matter as much as topic.

The cleanest research-based summary: **money is the most overweighted factor in job decisions, intrinsic interest and meaning are the most underweighted, and the real question in your case is whether "leading a team" is a genuine intrinsic goal of yours or an attractive-sounding status marker** — the literature suggests those two have very different outcomes.

- **Hedonic adaptation**: raises produce a satisfaction bump that decays substantially within about a year (Boswell et al.'s "honeymoon-hangover effect," 2005 — also relevant because it applies to job changes generally: satisfaction spikes after switching, then often drops below baseline).

## Intrinsic interest matters a lot

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically motivated work predict sustained well-being better than extrinsic rewards. There's also evidence extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: task significance, skill variety, and autonomy are robust predictors of satisfaction and low turnover.

## The complication in your case

The clean "interesting work vs. money" framing doesn't quite apply, because **leading a team is itself an intrinsic motivator** if it's a genuine long-held aspiration, not just a status marker. The research on management transitions is mixed, though: studies of individual contributors moving into management (and Gallup's manager research) suggest many people who *think* they want to manage find it less satisfying than expected, especially if the underlying work is boring — managers' satisfaction is strongly tied to whether they find the domain meaningful.

## Rough synthesis

answer:
There's actually a fair amount of relevant research here. Let me summarize the main findings.

## Pay matters less than people predict

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of 86 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is only about **r = .15** — surprisingly weak. People at high salaries are barely more satisfied with their jobs than people at modest ones.
- **Hedonic adaptation**: raises produce a satisfaction boost that fades quickly, often within a year (Clark et al. and related longitudinal panel studies on income changes). People adapt to a higher salary; it becomes the new baseline.
- **Herzberg's two-factor theory** (older but influential and broadly replicated in spirit): pay is a "hygiene factor" — low pay causes dissatisfaction, but high pay doesn't generate satisfaction. Satisfaction comes from "motivators": the work itself, achievement, responsibility, growth.

## Interesting/meaningful work matters a lot

- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan, decades of studies): intrinsic motivation — driven by autonomy, competence, and the inherent interest of the task — robustly predicts wellbeing, persistence, and performance. Extrinsic rewards predict these much more weakly and can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** meta-analysis on meaningful work: meaningfulness shows strong correlations with job satisfaction, engagement, and commitment, and negative correlations with intent to quit — effect sizes substantially larger than typical pay effects.
- **Nye et al. (2012)** meta-analysis: fit between your interests and your job predicts both satisfaction and performance.
- **Wrzesniewski's** work on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as intrinsically meaningful report higher life satisfaction than those oriented toward advancement or pay.

## Job changes and regret

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** — the "honeymoon-hangover effect": people who switch jobs experience a satisfaction spike, which then declines back toward their prior baseline within about a year. So the excitement of a new offer is a poor predictor of how you'll feel in year two.
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: long-term regrets are dominated by *inactions* — things people didn't try. **Roese & Summerville (2005)** found career is among the top life domains for regret. Interestingly, this cuts both ways for you: not pursuing leadership you've always wanted is exactly the kind of "road not taken" people regret — but so is abandoning work you found genuinely engaging.

## The complication in your case

The research framing of "money vs. interesting work" is too simple for your situation, because the Meta offer includes something with intrinsic value to you: **leading a team, which you say you've always wanted**. Responsibility and growth are Herzberg "motivators," not hygiene factors. SDT would classify a long-held aspiration to lead as potentially intrinsic, not extrinsic.

So the research-informed way to frame your decision is probably not "money vs. interest" (where research clearly favors interest) but **"interesting work vs. a leadership aspiration"** — where the salary bump is, per the evidence, nearly irrelevant. One relevant caveat: studies on engineers transitioning to management show mixed satisfaction outcomes, and *forecasted* enjoyment of a role is often inaccurate (Gilbert & Wilson's affective forecasting research). Wanting to lead and enjoying leading are correlated but not the same thing — if you can test leadership in a lower-stakes way first (e.g., mentoring or tech-leading within your current role), that would de-risk the inference.

answer:
Great question, and there's actually a decent body of research that bears on this. Let me summarize the most relevant findings:

## Pay and job satisfaction: weaker link than people expect

- **Judge et al. (2010)** conducted a meta-analysis of 86 studies and found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction was only **r ≈ .15**. Pay *increases* showed similarly weak relationships with satisfaction. The authors' conclusion: pay differences explain a surprisingly small share of how people feel about their jobs.
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (Frederick & Loewenstein, 1999; Diener et al.) shows people adapt quickly to salary increases — the satisfaction boost from a raise typically fades within months to a year, while job *content* is something you experience every day and adapt to less completely.
- Caveat: **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** and later **Killingsworth (2021)** show income does correlate with well-being, but the effects are largest at lower income levels. At Research Engineer compensation levels at OpenAI/Meta, you're far past the range where marginal salary differences predict much.

## Intrinsic motivation and work characteristics

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** has decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation — interest, autonomy, competence — predicts sustained engagement and well-being better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence for an **undermining effect**, where extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic interest over time.
- The **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)** and its meta-analyses (Humphrey et al., 2007) consistently find that skill variety, task significance, and challenge are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and lower turnover intention.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on "job/career/calling" orientations: people who view work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life satisfaction and lower absenteeism than those with career (advancement) or job (paycheck) orientations — even within the same occupation.

## Regret research — this cuts both ways for you

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. A counterargument to staying put by default.
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**: people's most enduring regrets concern failures to live up to their **"ideal self"** (aspirations, dreams) rather than their **"ought self"** (duties, sensible choices). This is directly relevant: which option represents your aspiration — leading a team, or doing the work you find fascinating? The research suggests *that's* the one whose loss you'd regret more.

## The leadership wrinkle

This is where it gets less clear-cut for your case:

- The desire to lead is itself a growth need. Research on **person-job fit** (Kristof-Brown et al., 2005 meta-analysis) shows that fit between what you want and what the job supplies ("needs-supplies fit") is the strongest fit-based predictor of satisfaction. If leading a team is a genuine long-held aspiration (which you say it is), that's not an "extrinsic" factor like salary — it may be intrinsic for you.
- However, research on transitions into management (e.g., studies on the engineer-to-manager pipeline) shows a substantial fraction of technical experts find management less satisfying than expected and some return to individual contributor roles — though selection effects make this literature messy.

## Honest summary

The research stacks up roughly like this: **the salary difference will likely matter very little; the interestingness of the work will likely matter a lot; the leadership opportunity is the genuine wildcard** — because it could be either an extrinsic status lure (predicts regret) or a core aspiration of your ideal self (predicts regret if you *don't* pursue it).

One limitation worth flagging: almost all of this is correlational or based on retrospective self-report. There's essentially no good experimental research on people choosing between concrete job offers, so apply appropriate uncertainty.

If you want to dig in, the Judge et al. (2010) meta-analysis ("The relationship between pay and job satisfaction") and Davidai & Gilovich (2018, "The ideal road not taken") are probably the two most directly useful reads.

answer:
Great question to ask before deciding. There's actually a fair amount of relevant research. Here's what I can recall — with the caveat that you should verify citations, as I'm reconstructing from memory:

## Pay vs. job satisfaction

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, a meta-analysis of ~90 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak: around **r = .15**. Pay *raises* produce a temporary boost that fades within roughly a year (hedonic adaptation).
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** famously found that emotional well-being plateaus around a certain income (~$75k in 2010 dollars, so higher now). At OpenAI/Meta compensation levels, you're far past the point where marginal salary affects day-to-day happiness much.
- **Kahneman & Schkade's "focusing illusion"** work is relevant: when comparing options, people systematically overweight salient, easily-comparable differences (like salary numbers) and underweight things that dominate actual daily experience.

## Interesting work and intrinsic motivation

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that autonomy, competence, and intrinsic interest predict sustained well-being, while extrinsically-motivated choices (money, status) predict worse outcomes. **Kasser & Ryan's** work found people who prioritize extrinsic goals report lower well-being even when they achieve them.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: skill variety, task significance, and meaningfulness are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction — much stronger than compensation in most studies.
- **Amabile & Kramer's "progress principle"** (diary studies of ~12,000 workday entries): the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was *making progress on meaningful work*. Not pay, not recognition.
- **Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on vocational interest fit: interest-job fit predicts satisfaction modestly and performance/persistence fairly well.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* far more than actions — paths not taken. Career regrets are consistently among the most common life regrets in surveys (alongside education and relationships).
- Notably, "I wish I'd taken that leadership opportunity" and "I wish I'd stayed with work I loved" are *both* common regret types — the research doesn't cleanly resolve your specific tradeoff.

## The honest complication in your case

The research framing of "interesting work vs. money" doesn't map perfectly onto your situation, because **leading a team is something you've always wanted** — that's an intrinsic goal too (growth, a different kind of competence), not just an extrinsic one. SDT would actually count that in Meta's favor. So your real tradeoff is between two intrinsic goods: task interest vs. a long-held developmental aspiration.

One more relevant finding: research on managers (e.g., Gallup data, and work by Benson, Li & Shue on promotions) suggests many people who move into management based on the *idea* of it find the day-to-day less satisfying than expected — worth testing whether you want to *lead* or want to have *become a leader*.

**Summary of what the literature would predict**: the salary difference will affect your happiness very little; the interestingness gap will affect it a lot and won't fade with adaptation the way money does; and the management question hinges on whether it's a genuine intrinsic pull or a status-tinted one.

## Regret research

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret **inactions** more than actions. This cuts both ways for you, interestingly — not taking the leadership opportunity you've "always wanted" could be the inaction you regret.

answer:
There's actually a fair amount of relevant research here. Let me summarize the main findings:

**Pay matters less than people expect**
- Judge et al. (2010), a meta-analysis of 86 studies, found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is only about r = .15. Pay *satisfaction* and actual pay are also weakly linked — people adapt.
- Research on hedonic adaptation (e.g., Clark et al., work on lead-lag effects of pay raises) shows that satisfaction boosts from raises largely dissipate within about a year. Anticipation of a raise often produces more happiness than having received it.
- Kahneman & Deaton (2010) found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus at moderately high incomes; Killingsworth (2021) revised this to show continued gains, but log-linear — meaning at OpenAI/Meta compensation levels, a "bit higher" salary predicts a negligible well-being difference.

**Intrinsic interest matters a lot**
- Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan) has decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation — doing work that's inherently interesting and autonomy-supporting — predicts well-being, persistence, and lower burnout, while extrinsically motivated choices (money, status) predict worse outcomes even when achieved (Kasser & Ryan, 1996; Sheldon & Krieger's longitudinal law-student studies are a striking example).
- Herzberg's classic two-factor theory: pay is a "hygiene factor" — low pay creates dissatisfaction, but high pay doesn't create satisfaction. Interesting work and achievement are "motivators" that do.
- Wrzesniewski et al. (1997): people who see work as a "calling" report substantially higher job and life satisfaction than those with "career" (advancement-focused) orientations, even within identical jobs.
- Amabile & Kramer's diary studies ("the progress principle"): the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work — not recognition or incentives.

**Regret research**
- Gilovich & Medvec (1995): long-term regrets are dominated by *inactions* — chances not taken — rather than actions.
- Roese & Summerville (2005): career is one of the top life-regret domains, alongside education.
- Davidai & Gilovich (2018): people's most enduring regrets concern failures to live up to their *ideal* self (aspirations, dreams) rather than their *ought* self (duties, obligations).

**One important complication for your case**
The regret literature cuts both ways for you. You said leading a team is "something I always wanted" — that's an ideal-self aspiration, and Davidai & Gilovich's findings suggest *unfulfilled aspirations* are exactly what people regret most. So the research doesn't cleanly say "stay with the interesting work"; it says "don't sacrifice the thing that's core to your aspirations for money."

Also relevant: research on promotion to management is mixed. Johnston & Lee (2013, "Extra Status and Extra Stress") found promotions temporarily boost job satisfaction but measurably *worsen* mental health, and the satisfaction boost fades. So if management appeals to you mainly as status/progression, the evidence is skeptical; if it appeals as a genuinely interesting new craft, that's different.

**The synthesis the research points to**
If you frame it as *interesting work vs. money*, the literature strongly favors interesting work — the salary delta will hedonically vanish in months, while daily intrinsic engagement compounds. If you frame it as *interesting work vs. a long-held aspiration to lead*, it's genuinely ambiguous, and the question becomes whether leading a team is intrinsically motivating to you or an "ought/status" goal. One empirical-ish test: would you want to lead a team doing the *less* interesting work, or do you want to lead a team doing *this* work? If the latter, the research suggests waiting for (or creating) a leadership path where you are may dominate both options.

answer:

## Pay vs. job satisfaction

Adaptation to the day-to-day *content* of work appears slower, because interesting work delivers ongoing novel experiences rather than a one-time level shift.

## Intrinsic interest and motivation

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** is probably the most robust framework here. Decades of studies show that autonomy, competence, and intrinsically motivated work predict sustained well-being, engagement, and lower burnout far better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence (the "undermining effect") that extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation.
- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997)** on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement — even within the same occupation.
- **Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model**: skill variety, task significance, and autonomy are core predictors of internal work motivation and satisfaction. Meta-analyses (Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007) confirm these hold up well.
- **Amabile & Kramer (2011, "The Progress Principle")**: analyzing ~12,000 diary entries, the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress in *meaningful* work — not recognition or incentives.

## Regret research (most relevant to your framing)

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions — but note this cuts both ways for you, since both options involve action; the relevant inaction would be "never trying management."
- **Davidai & Gilovich (2018)**: people's most enduring regrets concern failures to live up to their *ideal self* (aspirations, dreams) rather than their *ought self* (duties, expectations). This is interesting for your case: if leading a team is a genuine long-held aspiration ("something I always wanted to do"), that's ideal-self territory too — not just the interesting research.
- Career-regret studies specifically (e.g., surveys summarized by Roese & Summerville, 2005) consistently rank career among the top life regret domains, and the common themes are "didn't pursue what I found meaningful" and "played it safe" — more often than "took the lower-paying option."

## Two honest caveats

1. **Your situation has a confound the research doesn't cleanly address**: the Meta offer isn't just money — it's a *leadership opportunity you've explicitly wanted*. SDT would say leading a team can itself satisfy competence and autonomy needs. So this isn't purely "interesting vs. money"; it's "interesting work vs. a different kind of intrinsic goal." ## Pay vs. intrinsic interest

- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) has decades of evidence that intrinsic motivation (interest, autonomy, mastery) predicts sustained well-being, while extrinsic rewards can sometimes even *undermine* intrinsic motivation (the "overjustification effect" — though the size of this effect is debated).

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* more than actions. This cuts both ways for you — but it's often cited regarding chances not taken.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career and education are the top life domains for regret, and the underlying driver is regret over **unfulfilled opportunity** — people regret most where they perceived a path not taken.

## Job switching specifically

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon-hangover effect"**: job changers experience a satisfaction spike in the new job, followed by a decline back toward baseline within about a year. Worth knowing if part of the appeal is novelty.

Whether team leadership is a genuine interest of yours or a "should" is something the research can't answer — but it's probably the crux.

## Interesting/meaningful work

- **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)** — one of the most replicated frameworks in organizational psychology — shows that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are strong predictors of intrinsic motivation, satisfaction, and lower turnover.

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)**: decades of evidence that pursuing intrinsic goals (interest, mastery, contribution) predicts well-being better than extrinsic goals (money, status), and that extrinsic rewards can even crowd out intrinsic motivation.

## Adaptation: a key asymmetry

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005)** documented the **"honeymoon-hangover effect"**: job switchers show a satisfaction spike that decays back toward baseline within about a year — *unless* the intrinsic features of the work genuinely improved. We adapt quickly to pay and title; we adapt much less to whether the daily work itself is engaging.

## Regret research — this cuts both ways for you

## Pay and job satisfaction: weaker link than people expect

At OpenAI/Meta compensation levels, you're deep in the flat part of the curve.
- **Hedonic adaptation** research (going back to Brickman et al., 1978) shows people adapt to income changes quickly — raises typically stop affecting day-to-day mood within months.

## Interesting work: stronger and more durable effects

- **Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan)** is probably the most robust framework here. Intrinsic motivation (interest, enjoyment of the work itself) predicts sustained engagement, well-being, and persistence far better than extrinsic rewards. There's even evidence extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation.
- The **Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham)** identifies skill variety, task significance, and autonomy as core drivers of satisfaction — "interestingness" loads heavily on these.
- **Nye et al. (2012, 2017)** meta-analyses on *interest congruence* (fit between your interests and your job) found it predicts both performance and persistence, with meaningful effect sizes.
- **Wrzesniewski's** work on job/career/calling orientations: people who see their work as a calling report substantially higher life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement — even within the same occupation.

## Regret research — relevant to your framing

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994, 1995)**: in the long run, people regret *inactions* far more than actions. This cuts in an interesting way for you — "I never tried leading a team" is the kind of regret that compounds.
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**: career is one of the top life-regret domains, and regrets concentrate where people perceived *opportunity but didn't act*.

## The complication: you *want* to lead

Here's where a naive reading would mislead you. SDT's three needs are autonomy, **competence**, and relatedness — and growth toward goals you genuinely hold matters a lot. Research on **unfulfilled aspirations** (e.g., work building on Higgins' self-discrepancy theory) suggests a persistent gap between your actual and ideal self is itself a chronic drag on well-being. If leading a team is a genuine long-held aspiration rather than a status marker, that's not an "extrinsic" factor — it's intrinsic for you.

One counterweight: studies on transitions into management (e.g., Gallup's findings that companies often promote people who don't thrive as managers; research on first-time managers' stress) suggest the *anticipated* satisfaction from a leadership role is often miscalibrated, in both directions.

## How the research nets out

The literature would frame your choice as: **salary delta ≈ negligible long-term effect; interestingness ≈ strong, durable effect; leadership ≈ depends entirely on whether it's a genuine intrinsic aspiration or a "should."** The honest answer is research can't decide the leadership question for you — but it does say fairly clearly that "much less interesting work for somewhat more money" is, on average, a losing trade, and that the decision really hinges on how much the team-lead role is a true calling versus an attractive label.

## Pay vs. job satisfaction

## Intrinsic job characteristics

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**, a large meta-analysis on work design, found that **task variety, task significance, autonomy, and skill utilization** are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and motivation — substantially stronger than compensation.

Intrinsic motivation (interest in the work itself) predicts persistence, performance quality, and well-being better than extrinsic rewards. **Kasser & Ryan's** work on aspirations found that achieving intrinsic goals (growth, mastery) boosts well-being, while achieving extrinsic ones (money, status) does so much less.

- **Schkade & Kahneman's "focusing illusion"**: when comparing options, we overweight whatever dimension is salient in the comparison (here, probably salary and title) and underweight the day-to-day texture of experience — which, for a researcher, is largely "is the problem I'm staring at today interesting?"
